title: Electron Microscopy of Haloviruses

Box 751, Portland, OR 97207, USA ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":0,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"4uuk3","text":"2Biota Holdings Limited, 10/585 Blackburn Road, Notting Hill Victoria 3168, Australia ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":0,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"55tjs","text":"3Max Planck Institute of Biochemistry, Department of Membrane Biochemistry, Am Klopferspitz 18, 82152 Martinsried, Germany","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":0,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"4fq9q","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"7b1sm","text":"Standard negative stain EM works best on samples with low salt concentrations, but many haloviruses are stable only at high salt concentrations. If one uses high salt preparations, the salts can crystallize on the grid, occluding the particles and heating up the specimen. One way to overcome the problem is to first fix the sample using gluteraldehyde. Another problem is poor adsorption to plastic-coated grids (e.g., formvar). Pretreatment of the grids with poly-L-lysine can alleviate this issue. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"8tdfd","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"fmb9g","text":"The method for examining haloviruses was adapted from that described by Tarasov et al. Negative stain with uranyl acetate.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"imageblock","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"D86F88409636449DBCFFCC490B0F6BE8","height":null,"is_video":false,"mime":null,"original_name":"MAVE ch 6 fig 3.png","placeholder":"https://content.protocols.io/23c2xe.png","source":"https://content.protocols.io/23c2xe.png","width":null}}}}
steps: A sample of virus is placed on a sterile surface and the grid, plastic-coated side down, placed on the droplet for 1.5–2 min. 00:02:00
The grid is then placed, for 1–1.5 min, on a drop of freshly filtered 2% w/v uranyl acetate and excess stain removed with filter paper. 00:01:30
After air drying, grids may be examined by transmission electron microscopy, as described in guidelines (Fig. 3A, B, and C in guidelines). For more information see guidelines.
creator: Ken Stedman
